speaker: interviewer
text: --with Max Veffer. Tell me how you got food when you were in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Food-- that introduced us to Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH] And for some reason, my mom called her Auntie Fifi. She looked like a French actress, so she called her Aunt Fifi. Without her, we would have died-- simply-- because there was no way for us to get food. She worked for the Underground. She got us  the ration cards. So that way, we could get food. She would bring us weekly-- or sometimes two weeks-- but she would bring us food. And that's-- but food-- [LAUGHS] we didn't get too much. Because the food was-- in the morning, one slice of bread and maybe a half a cup of milk. And that was our breakfast. And then, maybe, at lunch, we may be given another slice of bread. And supper time, well, there wasn't much food around-- very little meat. But for whatever it was, my mother made. We got accustomed to have-- you know-- sugar beets. So what she would do is, she minced them like patties, like mincemeat. It tasted awful, but it fed your stomach. I hated them. It was too sweet for me. And but-- but they have it on potatoes. She kept the potato peels, because she minced them, again, because we didn't throw nothing away-- nothing away. And in the beginning, it wasn't too bad. But as you went along, we got pretty hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, could your mother cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We had a little stove. We had a little stove. And it was bad, because the smoke-- we couldn't get out-- you know. The smoke, you inhaled it. It wasn't very good. But Annie, before she used to go to school, we lay all-- we all laid down on the floor, and she aired out the room. She did it in the morning, and she would do it at night before we'd go to sleep. So that way, we get some little bit of fresh air. But as far as food, we had very little to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was as if she was cleaning out her own bedroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's correct. And actually, she-- there might be a neighbor that feel that she still slept in that room. You know what I mean? The windows are open. And she used to sing and look out the window, say hello to the neighbors. You know? So they knew that she was still in that room. And well, that's the way-- the daily chores was we'd get up in the morning and say, thank you, we're still here. And everybody finds themselves something to do. I took a liking in saving pictures of soccer players. I had a full-- I have a full album. I used to put them in there. And I put the stories in from the games. I-- I had a whole album. I kept that going. And when I didn't have to do that, I went down to my spot-- my post under-- near the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the pictures of the soccer players?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, paper-- daily paper. The daily--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who brought that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Annie. Yeah, we had the paper. She brought us magazines, you know. They looked-- they were-- that was pretty well-arranged. And maybe that was part of the reason we got through, because we kept ourselves occupied. You see? And if you keep your brain happy, that's-- that's-- my-- it was not-- physical too, because my brother Joe never got off the bed. I at least did my exercise. He was too lazy. And he suffers of it today. He has bad legs. But we kept busy. And that maybe got us through all this-- mentally. We were mentally very strong people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play any kind of games together, as a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad-- I wasn't much-- yes. He used to play games with my sister. My sister used to like card games, you know? And-- it kept her busy. Also, we had a map, you know, on the wall, and you know, we read off the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because in the-- when was the invasion? It started in 19-- what is it? 1943 that-- the Dieppe we had. And my father-- my father pinpointed everything on the map-- whatever was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they landed in Normandy, he says, oh. We'll soon be a free. Little did we knew, we had another three years to go-- at least almost two years to go. We mapped everything down, though. And we had a real bad experience one time. That was our first learning of bombing. We were having lunch, and the Germans-- the barracks-- where I went to school-- that's where the Germans were. So they tried to bomb that. And we didn't know anything. Before we knew it, there was a noise or something. We flew from one end of the room to the other. They bombed the school, but they never hit-- the-- they never hit the-- the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: They hit the house where my teacher of the sixth head-- what was his name? Tiesen. You see? Mr. Tiesen-- he came to his end there, because they blew that house away instead of the school. But that was a-- was our first experience with bombing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our other experience was-- my mom-- my mom-- whatever it was, there was something wrong with her heart. We took Dr. Martens-- very-- he came to see her. And for weeks, we couldn't move. The poor woman was in pain. But they couldn't take her out. So for weeks, we couldn't move. We couldn't talk. We couldn't-- we whispered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of her. She was in bed-- laying in bed. Because she was very ill.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you moved, that would make her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she didn't-- the-- the sound, you know? She was-- like, somebody's in intensive care. Nobody can see them. It was something like this-- something similar to that. She was in intensive care for weeks. But he gave her medicine, and it finally went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you know Dr. Martens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Martens-- we knew him from before the war. He was a customer of my dad. He lived just around the corner from us. And he was the director of the Catholic hospital-- a very, very nice man. He did a lot. He did a lot, that man.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that you could trust him-- that he could help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: How can I trust you? That's how it went. We trust. We had no other choice-- to trust people. We had no other choice. Should we trust him? And hopefully, it worked out OK. And it did. This man did wonders-- wonders.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next one was my sister. She had appendix. How can we take her out? So by rest-- we couldn't move-- again, the same thing over again. But with rest, the appendix went away. Right after the war, she had her appendix taken out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the plague came, for flea plague. I tell you, if you want to wish somebody bad, wish your enemies a flea plague-- not only our place, because we kept it clean-- the whole city. Fleas, I tell you, all over the beds and-- every night before we go to sleep, the war was on. Kill as many fleas as you can before you go to sleep. Otherwise, you get bitten up. We got bitten up, anyways. But as it came, that's how it went away.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the meantime, we felt like peasants, you know? How deeply-- how low-- I mean, you live-- have to live in a-- in a room like that. You can't move. And they were, oh-- I don't know where they came from. But they were there. How long did that last?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God, about a month. We were bitten. Oh! We were bitten. But we put-- we put vinegar, vinegar, oh, anything on your-- on your body to-- to-- it was itchy. Terrible. Never-- but that wasn't a good experience, I tell you. But we were always glad to see Auntie Fifi, even if she didn't bring nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you know Auntie Fifi?

speaker: interviewee
text: God, I think we got her through Dr. Martens. We got her through this. Even when she didn't bring something, she just came to say hello-- to keep up the spirits-- bring us the outside news. Mind you, Annie used to come up, too-- whatever what was new, or they-- mind you, there wasn't much news, except the Germans. The bad news was, next door-- Germans moved in. That was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That happened-- oh, it was almost near the end of the war, actually. That was in 19-- the war ended in '45. That was in '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two officers-- officers moved to the empty-- the house was empty, so they moved in. So we had Germans next door to us. Now we're there. [LAUGHS] You know what I'm saying?

speaker: interviewer
text: In a semi-detached?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, a house. It was a house next door.

speaker: interviewer
text: But detached?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I-- I kept pretty good track of them. They can't-- they don't know I'm in there. But in the meantime, we were there. And the next bad thing that happened-- you say, bad things can happen to you. Dr. Martens comes in one day. He says, you have to help us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's these two old fogeys that have got nowhere to go, and they need somewhere to stay. They'll only be good for two weeks. Sure. They stayed till the end of the war. They were two bad-- they were bad people. I got-- I don't-- I never hate anybody, but I sometimes dislike. I disliked these two people. One had sugar-- it was a diabetes. And she had bronchitis. And they-- the only place they slept-- they had to sleep upstairs. There was another room upstairs, and they got that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they had to eat with us-- gross. Gross. I don't want to say this, but he was gross. Because he coughed like-- terrible. And we children, we used to talk about it. Oh, this is a dirty old man. And he was. And we couldn't do nothing. Doctor Martens, when are you going to take these people away? They stayed to us until the end of the war. It was-- instead of 8, it was 10. But that wasn't the bad-- the worst of it. There was another raid. How are you going to go with 8, 10 people, and where there's only room for 8? Here's how we managed. The raid came. I says, what's going to happen? There was a friend next door, of Makkinje's. His name is Bakker-- Hank Bakker. He says, there is a garage-- they lived above a old garage that they used to repair cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the old days, they didn't lift the cars. They used to drive them over a-- a-- a hole. And the guy is inside underneath and fixes the car that way. But there's no more cars there. There was a-- a building material-- like cement bags, and all that sort of thing. We'll take Shelley, and Joe, and Alan, and we put them in there. And we cover it up, so they won't know they're there. OK, that's good. And we'll take the old people, in there. We decided that before. That wasn't decided the day of the raid, but that was decided before. I said it so very calmly, but we were in a little panic, because we didn't know what to do with these two old people. And if he coughs-- we're gone. You can't say nothing. If he coughs-- and he was always coughing-- [COUGHS] --always coughing.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father says, listen, Mr. Aaldwereld. You cough, you're dead. OK? We go in there. And you know how we kept him quiet? Guess how we kept him quiet. He had a knife in his hand, and he kept it to his throat. He never coughed. The Germans were there for five minutes. He never coughed, never coughed. The minute he cut off he started coughing for an hour. You tell it-- it's such joke. But it is not a joke. He said, Mr. Aaldwereld, if you cough, you're dead. OK? And that's how we got through that raid. She'd say it so simply, but it seemed like hours. Those raids were like-- you know. And that was the only two raids we got through, with the old people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as time went along, she-- she had a-- almost a nervous breakdown, Mrs. Makkinje. She said, I can't take this no more. Because she was very nervous because of those raids. And she had a son. And she kept it from the son that we were there. That kid never knew that we were there. He lived in the same--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was the son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years old. But he never knew. We found out after the war, he says, I knew-- six years old. I knew. And you never told? He said, no, no. I'm not supposed to tell. Did your mommy tell that we were there? No, no, no. But I knew. I hear noise.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know Annie's not sleeping in her own room. Annie's sleeping downstairs on the couch. So who was in the room? Nobody? But I never told. And he didn't. The kid never told. That's a-- that's a-- that's a miracle, by itself. A six-year-old shouldn't talk to anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: But she used to yell at this man. And he was a-- he used to come up. you know, he liked talking to us. I don't know whether she was jealous or something, but-- there was one day she says, I want to get rid of these people. I can't take this anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, oh. My dad used to calm her down. The war will be over soon. Yeah, the war will be over soon. But in the meantime, I have you people here. OK? So he always said, but you're getting paid. And that kept her down.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one day, we hear her yelling-- God! On the top of her voice-- I want to get out of here! I'm going to kill myself! I'm going to tell on these people. I'm going to get rid of-- and she ran out of the house. Now, we can't stay here. Suppose she didn't-- but we didn't know that. So went with [? Bea ?] Bakker.

speaker: interviewer
text: She could have gone--

speaker: interviewee
text: She could have gone to the Gestapo and tell them that she wants us to get out of there. So we talked it over with Hank Bakker-- nice people. He said well, come-- come to us for a few nights and see what happens. A few nights became till the end of the war. The whole family moved into Hank Bakker. If it wasn't for the Gentile people, we would never be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: We forgot to catch up with Shelley, and Alan, and Joe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, thank you for reminding me. We forgot all about them. Like I just forgot about them. Those poor kids were in there for over an hour. We forgot. By the time they got-- we got them out-- they were full of cement. Because they tried to get out. They removed some of it. But kids, how can you remove? They put back the cement on top of the-- they put some planks and put sand cement.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time they got-- they were full of cement. But we got them out. But we forgot about them. But my dad says, where is Shelley? Oh my God! We forgot about them! But thank God. If we didn't, they'd have got out-- for days they smelled from cement. Yeah. But then-- like you said, everything ended well. While you were in hiding at the Makkinje's how did you wash?

speaker: interviewee
text: We washed-- we washed-- there were showers around. Everybody had a turn. We put up a curtain because my sister had to wash herself. My mom has to wash herself. So we put up a curtain. And we have a big tub. We fill it up with water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was water. Oh, yeah. We would get the water. We go downstairs with a pail. We fill a big pail with the water, come upstairs. It's a lot of work, but that's what we had to do. Because there was no washroom upstairs. There was no washroom. We had to go downstairs. Oh, yeah. We filled it up, and that's how we washed. Good question. [LAUGHS] Yeah, so that's how we got washed, all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about toilet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toilet-- we came downstairs. There's a toilet downstairs. Yeah, we had to go downstairs to the toilet. And God forbid, she had-- sometimes her friends come to visit. I wanted to go out. I had to go so badly. I had to go so badly, I go behind the curtain. Pardon my expression. We had a little toilet there, a little potty. And you go into it. And you can't hide. I can't do it in my pants. So we had to do it. And everybody had to do the same thing, because they get relatives visiting them. The days-- the days- on the weekends, they get relatives. We couldn't move nowhere. We just sit. I can't even sit on a staircase, because they stay there. They can't say, you can't come.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from-- from the time that the people come in, we had to be quiet-- can't move. You stay in one spot, because even when you walk, there's cracks in the floor that-- you know, you can hear there's somebody upstairs. We don't move. We have everything ready-- food ready on your plate for the whole day. Everything ready so you don't have to move. And everybody sits. So what I used to do, I sleep the day away. You can't move. What can I tell you? It's as simple as that. And we had many, many, many days like that, because they have relatives and friends. They come in. Yeah. Were there ever any Jewish holidays that you observed in hiding? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, that's also a good question. I don't think my mind-- our minds was-- our minds weren't on that for a simple reason. We had a one-track mind-- how can we survive? One-track mind-- I don't think there was-- I mean we-- we prayed a lot in our own little way. Because there was a lot of nights that I come from-- come away from the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I cried myself to sleep, because I can't go out. Why can't I? I could never understand why were we picked on. Why us? I mean, we never did anything wrong to anybody. Why should we have to live like this? And many nights, I slept-- I-- I cried myself to sleep. Because my dad didn't have an answer for it. My mother didn't have an answer. Nobody has an answer for that, even today. Who has an answer for that? Nobody has an answer for that. What about schooling? Did any of you try and keep up your education?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did a lot of reading. We had a few books. But see, this is a hard thing to explain. Your brain is so full of trying to think good things-- see, bad things come in your brain, and I cancel them. I cancel. Any time I have a bad thought, I would cancel it out. And that's the way we-- it's like you can almost think, it's like a jail sentence. And the bad things, you cancel out. You think about nice things. My mom always said, think about nice things. If you think about nice things, the days will go by good. If you think about bad things, then you're going to have a bad time getting through the day. To learn was work-- to keep your brain-- to work. And I don't think we had the mental strength to go through say, two and two is four, and go through lessons. At least, I don't think we did much. We did a lot of reading. But to put your brains to learning-- I couldn't make myself to do this. I have no explanation for it. Maybe a psychiatrist [LAUGHS] or whatever-- has an explanation for that. But we couldn't get ourselves to do that. At least, I don't remember that anybody-- any of us did.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to talk to each other about nice things-- what we're going to do if we go out, and the nice things that we used to do-- that type of thing we used to keep ourselves amused with. You know what I'm saying? We had to keep ourselves amused. If you would get-- if you would start-- yourself thinking about work, we would get almost depressed. Because we couldn't do it outside. We have-- why would we have to do it here?

speaker: interviewee
text: With pleasant things, you don't think-- you think about something nice. So therefore, it will be easy to keep your brain happy with something nice, than something learning. Maybe it sounds strange, but that's the way I remembered it. That's the way I remembered it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you day dreamed. What were your dreams of what would happen after, when you were free?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's also a very good question. I'm 16. Now, I was a fast learner. I mean, you're talking about something-- and I would look-- girls. I'm not interested in boys. But I'm interested in girls. And there was some nice girls passing by. And I says, one of these-- when I come out, I'm going to make up for lost time. And I did. But that's one thing, I kept myself going. When I come out, I'm going to go back to play soccer. My dream was playing for the-- the Dutch national team. That was my dream. It never came true. I played, but I never played for the national team. But that was my dream that kept me happy. And all sorts of nice things. You kept yourself busy daydreaming about nice things. And that's what kept you going. The minute you start thinking about something bad, you get depressed. And depressed is very bad. If you can't get out-- with the four walls-- you're sitting between four walls-- it's bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I came out of the war-- and I'm getting ahead of me-- bad stomach. We all wound up with bad stomachs-- nervous stomachs. I got-- I got a hiatus hernia. That's a hard word for me to say-- hiatus hernia. Is that correct? Yeah, well that's what I got. I got a bad back. But I'm alive. So we're thankful for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's-- yes. That's-- I hope that answers your question. That's how we got through the days, because like I said, learning-- it would have been a good thing to do. But we didn't. But it worked out OK. I was a businessman for 45 years. So I don't think I did so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have false papers-- papers in other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have to. What for?

speaker: interviewer
text: But the ration cards were in what name-- that Auntie Fifi--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were in anybody's name. It didn't matter. She could pick them up on her own name and from different people. Oh, yeah. They were-- she had no problem with that. That woman was fantastic with that. We got so hungry when-- I mean, what is two slices of bread a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: The nicest part was when-- when-- when-- the-- the Red Cross dropped parcels. They dropped parcels. Each parcel had a white bread, which we hadn't seen in two years. And there was a quarter of a pound of butter, if I remember correctly-- cheese, a little bit of cheese-- and it was, you know. And Auntie Fifi brought these in-- one for each. It was like somebody brought in a feast. My mother says, now, slice it nicely so you have-- I said, are you kidding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took a knife. I sliced the whole bread, like this big, I remember. I smeared all the butter on it, put all the cheese on it. And I set myself back in the corner and ate the whole thing. You're going to get sick, she says. I don't care. It will be well worth it. And I ate the whole bread in one shot. Oh, yeah-- white bread-- we hadn't seen white bread for three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I went to the toilet a lot, but I didn't get sick. [LAUGHS] No, that was a feast. The Red Cross-- a lot of these parcels were-- there was a-- there was a-- I think, from heaven. It was good. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You kids didn't fight over food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, never. A matter of fact, the days that I wasn't that hungry-- and I saw my kid brother-- and he was so hungry-- I gave him mine, half of mine. Because I couldn't see him be so hungry. Yeah. There was-- there was not much to eat-- potato peels, potatoes. There was always potato and sugar beets. Oh, that sits in my brain like a sentence. We made everything from sugar beets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sometimes you slept, to miss a meal so that you wouldn't have to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah. Well, what we used to do a lot of times, when we didn't get enough to eat the next day, we slept till like-- like a half a day. When you sleep, you don't need-- you don't need to eat. You know? That was-- that's how we got through the day, sometimes. Because there just wasn't any. People are people. Even us, even the people outside-- there were people starving from-- from-- from-- from hunger. There was nothing to eat. The Germans took it all. Whatever food there was, they-- they cleaned the wholesales out. I mean, I would always have plenty to eat-- vegetables. The vegetables were still there. And the potatoes were still there. Everything was still there. But they haul it away in trucks-- truckloads, to feed their-- to feed their army. They couldn't care less if we lived or died. That could be Jews, or it could be-- there was no Jews, as far as they were concerned. There was no Jews, so it was Gentile people that died. I mean, they picked the-- the Gentile people up to go to work in the concentration just as much as they did the Jewish people. They didn't care, especially at the end of the war. They didn't care who they took. They took children and old men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of your family, people were taken-- of your larger family?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my mother's side-- 1, 2. 3 brothers gone. One had three children. And on my father's side, three brothers-- four-- eight children. Two children are still alive. They're living in Toronto-- on my mother's side. [SIGHS] But the rest has all gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. We never heard from them. And the people that came back, came back. But most-- there's not too many people that came back from concentration camps. From there, they were like my-- my-- the same as my age. No, the people that went to concentration camps, you could say goodbye to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say-- I would say-- maybe 10% came back, out of the concentration camp. And it's terrible to say, because they would have been all my age, and they would be married with children. Because there were a lot of grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what camps they were taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. How would we know? Who to tell us? No. I still have visions, sometimes, as you see people come back after so many years. I always wished that I had amnesia, so-- so they would remember-- the memory would come back. And you look-- you know,

speaker: interviewee
text: I look sometimes at these pictures from-- they show these war pictures. But it's a bad thing. I sit there crying, because you're looking for faces. You know what I'm saying? And I see sometimes-- maybe I see a face that I know. You know? But no. It's sad. It's so sad that those people were wasted. They was wasted.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always say, if Hitler would have left the Jewish people alone, he would have ruled the world. That's the truth. That's a true story. I'm telling you. If he would have left the Jewish people alone, he would have ruled the world. He kept himself too busy with the Jews, so he lost the war because of that. That's why-- I personally believe that. He lost the war because he killed so many people-- innocent people. And that's what I believe in. So it's sad. They didn't come back. Now we try to build a new family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm blessed with good kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a--
